title: The Gate of the Silent Six
Becoming Through the Thread
medium: Acrylic on canvas, beads and threads
description: A shadow looms. A gray demon begins to change. Another emerges — stained-glass blue, sewn into form, framed in sacred thread. This is not dialogue, but transformation. The embroidered flowers, distant on the canvas, recall the outside world. A reflection on fear turned into shape, and the labor of becoming.
symbolism: A visual ritual of inner transfiguration. Three figures mirror one being across time: a vast shadow representing unconscious origin, a shapeless gray demon caught mid-transition, and a fully formed twin — vibrant, embroidered, adorned with beads like cathedral glass. His presence is contained in a stitched blue frame — not confinement, but completion. Their outstretched hands don't touch in greeting but in transmission. This is not two demons — it is one soul passing through layers of self-conflict. Elsewhere on the canvas, embroidered flowers bloom in silence — removed from the core struggle, they speak of memory, belief, or temptation. Religious symbols hide in thread and structure, suggesting salvation not from the divine, but from oneself. To truly change, you must stitch yourself together, one sacred wound at a time.

title: The Grid of Fate: Tic-Tac-Toe
description: A blue demon floats in a dreamlike space, eyes embedded in its hands. One limb forms a dove — a fragile symbol of peace. A grid of tic-tac-toe overlays the scene. Fate, fear, and inner games merge into a quiet confrontation.
symbolism: A meditation on inner tension, fate, and the illusions of control. A blue demon hovers in a submerged, suspended world — eyes embedded in its hands, one morphing into a dove, symbolizing the fragile desire for peace. Transparent circles drift like thoughts or souls rising through water. Over them, a tic-tac-toe grid hovers — not playful, but existential. The game is never won, only replayed. This demon doesn't threaten — it watches. It asks: are we playing against others, or against our own shadow? And will we choose to resist, or to reconcile?

title: The Interweaving Paths
description: Two mirrored demons move in opposite directions — one backward, one forward — their heads shaped like cosmic rings. The canvas splits between movement and silence, memory and forgetting. A visual meditation on time, duality, and the paths we carry within.
symbolism: An exploration of duality, memory, and the non-linear flow of time. Two identical demons are fused through a shared trajectory — their heads forming an armillary-like structure, echoing ancient models of the cosmos. One looks back, one moves forward, yet both are suspended in a single unified orbit. Half the canvas breathes with circular, kinetic brushwork — a rhythm of cycles and return. The other half fades into white, silent and emptied, as if time itself pauses. Dotted marks scatter across the work like celestial coordinates, mapping an emotional constellation. How do we move forward while carrying what was — and how even our mirrored selves may be searching for the same light?

title: Transcendent Glow
description: From a crimson gothic window, two left glass hands stretch outward — identical, but mirrored wrong, like a sacred error. They open not to act, but to receive. Light passes through them from within. A soft yellow glow emerges from grayness; five pale candleholders hover above. A vision of vulnerability as power, and revelation through imperfection.
symbolism: A moment of awakening not through force, but through surrender. Two large left hands — both from the same side, both open and made of glass — emerge from a crimson gothic form, like a cathedral fragment or sacred wound. Their mirrored imperfection is intentional: left hands are tied to intuition, vulnerability, and the subconscious. Here, they multiply — rejecting action, embracing reception. Through their transparency, the glowing red temple is visible, as if the soul of the structure shines through its fragile body. The yellow light in the gray field suggests emergence, not dominance. Above, five white candleholders hover — perhaps symbols of the five senses, five thresholds, or memory itself. This piece is about sacred imbalance, inner exposure, and transformation that comes when we stop trying to control what we are becoming.

title: Knight
description: A knight merges with a white gothic temple, his black armor blooming with white flowers. Flesh, steel, and stone dissolve into one. No longer a warrior, he becomes part of the sanctuary — a vision of sacrifice and sacred stillness.
symbolism: The inner warrior not in motion, but in surrender. A black-armored figure dissolves into a white gothic window — part steel, part sacred architecture. His armor, once a barrier, now blossoms with white flowers: symbols of purity, remembrance, and quiet death. He is no longer a knight in battle but a relic, a statue within his own cathedral. The armor becomes a reliquary — a container for memory, loss, and silence. We become what we protect. The knight merges with the sacred, but at the cost of self. He guards not with sword, but with stillness. The temple is not outside him — it is him.

title: Sphinx
description: A towering sphinx looms behind a gothic window. Violet orchids stretch two hands — one to the past, one toward the sacred. The flowers drift in spirals, like fading time. A meditation on memory, eternity, and loss.
symbolism: An ancient, overwhelming presence placed behind a small gothic frame. The contrast of scale disrupts perception: the divine no longer fits within sacred architecture. Two ethereal hands emerge from violet orchids — one reaches back into the spiral of dissolving petals, the other toward the temple. A dialogue between rooted memory and forward-seeking faith. The circular motion of the flowers evokes time as a force pulling everything into forgetting. A quiet ruin, where beauty and enormity conflict.

title: Transformation
description: From demonic hands grows a tree. A green retro car moves through a temple and a chair, dissolving in shadow. Above, a ghost or dragon stirs. A visual myth of decay and becoming.
symbolism: A layered tale of decay, memory, and slow rebirth. A tree grows from demonic hands — nature breaking from darkness. A green vintage car fades through stages: rear to front, past to present, crossing through domestic objects and sacred remnants. The gothic temple holds an odd relic — a tire — a strange idol of forgotten motion. Above, a spectral dragon looms. All forms here are shifting: vehicle, space, spirit, life. A story told not in events, but in fragments of stillness.

title: Sunset
description: Fingers emerge from a woman's eyes; a spiral stair descends from her mouth. A knight's head is a vase, birthing six hands and a demon. Red lilies bloom across the scene. A ritual of grief, beauty, and broken resurrection.
symbolism: A twilight liturgy of endings. A woman's head fuses with a gothic frame. From her eyes, fingers emerge — an invasion of identity. A demonic hand rests inside her. Her mouth spirals into a stair: descent, escape, or internal fall? Before her, a knight stands headless — in place of his head, an ancient vase holds six human hands and a demon's palm. Red lilies punctuate the frame, blooming like blood offerings. It is a funeral of forms, and a strange grace within decay.

title: Blue Temple
description: A staircase leads from a blue temple to an open demonic hand. A massive female body intertwines with the architecture. Sword-halves act as clock hands. An invitation to merge flesh, spirit, and sacred geometry.
symbolism: A study in luminous embodiment. From the temple emerges a stair — not to ascend, but to approach the waiting demonic hand, inviting transformation. A towering feminine form dances into the frame, fusing with the architecture like breath through stone. On either side, sword-halves echo the face of time. The painting becomes a clock, a threshold, and a hymn to union.

title: Metamorphose
description: A demon atop a gothic arch, with four arms and a triangular head. Below, a carousel of horse transformation: one sucked in, one emerging as skeleton. A meditation on identity's collapse and rebirth.
symbolism: A ritual of shape and spirit. At the summit of a grand gothic frame stands a demon: feminine, four-armed, balanced between grace and chaos. A spiral staircase leads down to a circle where horses shift — one dissolves, another forms from bone. To the left, a skeletal equine emerges into life with a demon's hand for a head. It is a cycle of form, where what breaks becomes sacred again.

title: Eve
description: A woman with two heads, a golden key between them. Her body is metallic, winged, and triply armed. Five cosmic arms reach out, three demonic. A vision of primal force and layered identity.
symbolism: A reimagining of origin and duality. The central figure, forged of metal, holds two faces divided by a key — choice, potential, and knowledge. Three arms gesture toward the cosmos, while from the galaxy behind her, five hands reach forward — three of them demonic. She is surrounded, offered, hunted, or crowned. Her wings speak of divinity, but her iron skin denies fragility. This is not innocence lost — it is power unveiled.

title: Temple II
description: A pink temple as portal. One side, a demon reaches for it; the other, what passed through became geometry — cube, cone, sphere. A visual meditation on how essence is transformed by passage.
symbolism: Soft-colored but philosophically sharp. The temple is not just structure but function: a converter. What passes through becomes form — simplified, defined, bound. A demon reaches for this transformation, perhaps to interrupt it. The image questions whether enlightenment purifies or flattens. Through the beautiful, the unknown is given shape — but also limit.

title: Temple I
description: A cathedral window opens to a staircase from which a demon descends. On either side, knight-demons with swords guard the threshold — one crowned with demonic hands, the other white, his head a torch of stars. Background: shattered stained glass.
symbolism: Conflict at the gates of transformation. The shattered stained glass reflects spiritual fracture; the two knights represent the soul's core duality — violence and transcendence, shadow and light, standing eternal guard at the threshold between worlds.

title: Zubeneschamali
description: A green-toned celestial ballet: a demon dances, surrounded by fragments of itself — three hands and a disassembled double head. Title references the Libra constellation, connecting cosmic balance to inner fragmentation.
symbolism: The dance of duality; cosmic law versus internal chaos. Echoes of incense, altars, and stellar symmetry bridge the sacred and the astronomical. What appears scattered is still in orbit — fragmentation as its own form of balance.
